theory: DWSIM is a multiplatform, CAPE-OPEN compliant chemical process simulator that allows for the modeling of process plants using rigorous thermodynamic and unit operations models. It is free/libre and open-source, allowing for inspection of code during execution. Its thermodynamics engine is available as a standalone dynamic link library (DLL) for integration with other applications.

topic: User Guide Contents and Software Overview
theory: DWSIM is an open-source chemical process simulator released under the GNU General Public License (GPL) version 3. It includes tools for process modeling (flowsheeting), sensitivity studies, flowsheet optimization, and characterization of petroleum fractions.

theory: The simulation workflow in DWSIM consists of configuring the chemical environment (compounds, property packages, and units) followed by flowsheeting, which involves connecting material and energy streams to unit operations such as mixers, splitters, separator vessels, tanks, and pipe segments. Calculation methods for pipe segments and output parameters for streams define the thermodynamic and physical state of the process.

DWSIM provides a variety of unit operations and reactor models including PipeSegment, Valve, Pump, Compressor/Expander, Heater/Cooler, Shortcut Column, Rigorous Column (Distillation/Absorption), Heat Exchanger, Air Cooler, Component Separator, Orifice Plate, Custom Unit Operation, Solids Separator (Continuous Cake Filter), Excel Unit Operation, Flowsheet Unit Operation, and renewable energy components such as PEM Fuel Cells, Water Electrolyzers, Hydroelectric Turbines, Wind Turbines, and Solar Panels. Reactor modeling involves defining input/output parameters and specific calculation methods.

The software supports automation via COM/API interfaces and is compatible with the CAPE-OPEN subsystem, allowing for external property packages and unit operations.

A Property Package consists of a set of methods and models for phase equilibrium and property calculation.

theory: A Property Package consists of methods and models for calculating physical and chemical properties of material streams, including thermodynamic models (EOS or hybrid) and methods for specific properties like surface tension. Peneloux Volume Translation is a correction factor applied to EOS-calculated liquid densities. Phase equilibrium calculations determine the distribution of components across vapor, liquid, and optionally solid phases.

theory: Phase equilibria calculations can use different numerical methods like Nested Loops, Inside-Out, or Gibbs Minimization. In a flash calculation, Gibbs energy should always decrease when there is a phase split. Supercritical compounds and LLE mixtures at low temperatures may require specific phase identification algorithms to avoid being incorrectly identified as VLE. Most thermodynamic models rely on binary interaction parameters fitted to experimental data.

theory: Thermodynamic models are selected based on fluid properties and conditions: Raoult's Law for low-pressure non-polar gases (<10 atm); EOS (PR, SRK) for high-pressure non-polar gases; PRSV2 for high-pressure polar gases; Chao-Seader, Grayson-Streed, or LKP for high Hydrogen content; CoolProp for refrigeration; NRTL/UNIQUAC/UNIFAC for polar chemicals; and Seawater for salt systems. Most models rely on binary interaction parameters (IPs) fitted to experimental data. DWSIM allows for regression of experimental data to calculate these parameters. Units systems can be changed mid-simulation with immediate effect.

theory: The DWSIM simulation window consists of several functional areas: Menu Bar (file handling and settings), Flowsheet Objects Palette (objects for the PFD), Flowsheet Objects List (searchable list), Material Streams window (calculated properties), Flowsheet window (editing area), Information window, Spreadsheet window (math operations), Charts window, and Script Manager (Python automation). Flowsheet objects include: Material Streams (matter limits and properties), Energy Streams (energy limits), Mixers (combining up to three material streams with mass/energy balances), Energy Mixers (combining up to three energy streams), and Splitters (mass balance units).

DWSIM utilizes several unit operations for flowsheeting: EnergyStream represents energy transfer; Mixer/Splitter handle mass and energy balances for stream combining/dividing; Valve calculations are based on isenthalpic expansion; Pipe simulates mono or two-phase flow using Darcy (single-phase), Beggs & Brill, or Lockhart & martinelli correlations; Pump, Compressor, and Expander models use user-defined efficiencies for isenthalpic or isentropic processes; SeparatorVessel divides vapor and liquid phases; Reactors include Conversion, Equilibrium, PFR, and CSTR types; Columns range from simple Shortcut models to rigorous thermodynamic Distillation, Absorption, and Refluxed Absorber models; OrificePlate is used for flow metering; and extensibility is provided via CustomUnitOperation (Python) and CAPE-OPEN sockets.

Logical operations include Controllers (matching variables), Specifications (functional dependencies), Recycles (material and energy), InputBoxes (quick property changes), and Switches. Material streams represent mass flow between operations.

theory: DWSIM is a sequential modular process simulator where calculations are performed on a per-module basis. The calculator checks for defined properties and propagates data downstream. A Sensitivity Study allows users to analyze the influence of up to two independent variables on dependent flowsheet variables by varying them across a defined range of points.

theory: Sensitivity analysis determines the influence of independent variables (like temperature and pressure) on dependent flowsheet variables by calculating values across a range of equally spaced points. Multivariate optimization handles single and multivariate problems with or without bound constraints, iteratively solving until the variation in the objective function is below a specified tolerance.

Utilities provide additional information like true critical point and phase envelopes, and their data can be used in sensitivity analysis and optimization studies.

theory: Utilities calculate properties for one selected object, such as True Critical Point, Phase Envelope, and Petroleum Cold Flow Properties (cetane index, flashpoint, refraction index). DWSIM classifies chemical reactions into three types: Conversion (specified as a function of temperature), Equilibrium (characterized by an equilibrium constant K), and Kinetic/Heterogeneous Catalytic (led by a velocity expression as a function of concentration). Reactions are managed in Sets; reactors use the Set as a parameter rather than individual reactions.

theory: Data regression determines interaction parameters for thermodynamic models by minimizing the difference between calculated and experimental values. The utility supports Txy and Pxy data types and uses optimization methods like Least Squares. Vapor phase fugacity can be treated as ideal or calculated via Peng-Robinson EOS. Currently, generated binary parameters must be exported manually to simulations or databases.

theory: Data regression in DWSIM involves using optimization methods to find optimum binary interaction parameters. The Least Squares (min T/P) objective function minimizes differences between calculated and experimental pressures/temperatures. Least Squares (min y) minimizes differences in vapor phase mole fractions. Least Squares (min T/P+y) minimizes both. Data regression files use the .dwrsd extension. Interaction parameters calculated via regression are not automatically copied to BIP databases and must be entered manually.

theory: DWSIM covers diverse domains: bioprocessing (ADM1, fermentation), environmental (RO, MSF, brine management), and consumer goods. theory: Material Stream: used to represent matter which enters and leaves the limits of the simulation and passes through the unit operations. Energy Stream: used to represent energy which enters and leaves the limits of the simulation and passes through the unit operations. Mixer: used to mix up to three material streams into one, while executing all the mass and energy balances. Splitter: mass balance unit operation - divides a material stream into two or three other streams. Valve: works like a fixed pressure drop for the process, where the outlet material stream properties are calculated beginning from the principle that the expansion is an isenthalpic process. Pipe: simulates a fluid flow process (mono or two-phase). The pipe implementation in DWSIM provides the user with various configuration options, including heat transfer to environment or even to the soil in buried pipes. Two correlations for pressure drop calculations are available: Beggs and Brill and Lockhart and martinelli. Both reduce to Darcy equation in the case of single-phase flow. Pump: used to provide energy to a liquid stream in the form of pressure. The process is isenthalpic, and the non-idealities are considered according to the pump efficiency. Separator Vessel: used to separate the vapor and liquid phases of a stream into two other distinct streams. Compressor: used to provide energy to a vapor stream in the form of pressure. The ideal process is isentropic (constant entropy) and the non-idealities are considered according to the compressor efficiency. Expander: the expander is used to extract energy from a high-pressure vapor stream. The ideal process is isentropic (constant entropy) and the non-idealities are considered according to the expander efficiency. Heater: simulates a stream heating process. Cooler: simulates a stream cooling process. Conversion Reactor: simulates a reactor where conversion reactions occur. Equilibrium Reactor: simulates a reactor where equilibrium reactions occur. PFR: simulates a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR). CSTR: simulates a Continuous-Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR). Shortcut Column: simulates a simple distillation column with approximate results using shortcut calculations. Distillation Column: simulates a distillation column using rigorous thermodynamic models. Absorption Column: simulates an absorption column using rigorous thermodynamic models. Heat Exchanger: simulates a countercurrent heat exchanger using rigorous thermodynamic models. Component Separator: model to simulate a generic process for component separation.

The Separator Vessel (flash drum) separates liquid from vapor phases using Adiabatic, Legacy, Heating/Cooling Isobaric, or Heating/Cooling Isothermic modes. The Tank simulates a fixed pressure drop where the outlet temperature remains the same and properties are calculated via TP Flash. Pipe Segments simulate fluid flow and pressure drop using correlations.

theory: The Pipe Segment unit operation simulates fluid flow using incremental mass and energy balances. The algorithm uses three nested loops: an external loop iterating on sections/increments, a middle loop for temperature, and an internal loop for pressure. Pressure drop is calculated based on fluid properties at the arithmetic mean of inlet and outlet conditions. Heat transfer is calculated using the equation Q = UAΔT_ml, where Q is heat transferred, A is external surface area, and ΔT_ml is the logarithmic mean temperature difference.

DWSIM provides cross-platform chemical process simulation using various thermodynamic models (EOS: Peng-Robinson, PC-SAFT, GERG-2008; Activity Models: NRTL, UNIQUAC, UNIFAC) and unit operations (Distillation, Reactors, Heat Exchangers). Desktop versions support CAPE-OPEN and Dynamic Modeling; mobile versions require Model Packs for advanced EOS and specific unit ops.

steps: Read properties from the fluid entering the section.
Calculate emulsion viscosity using volume fractions of liquid phases (Equations 10.2-10.7).
Calculate slurry viscosity based on solid volume fraction and relative viscosity (Equations 10.8-10.10).
Initialize heat balance for the section with an estimate for outlet temperature T2 based on external temperature.
Begin temperature convergence loop.
Calculate pressure drop for the section using Beggs-Brill, Lockhart-Martinelli, or Petalas-Aziz models (defaults to Darcy-Weisbach for single phase).
Calculate outlet pressure P2 and iterate for convergence, applying a secant procedure after three iterations.
Calculate Overall Heat Transfer (Step 8).

theory: The calculation for pipe segments in DWSIM involves iterative mass and heat balances performed incrementally. Heat transfer is modeled as Q = UA∆Tml. Viscosity is adjusted for emulsions (using liquid phase fractions) and slurries (using solid volume fractions). Pressure drop models include Beggs-Brill, Lockhart-Martinelli, and Petalas-Aziz, while Darcy-Weisbach is used for single-phase flow: ∆P = f * (L/D) * (v^2 / 2g). Friction factors (f) are determined based on the Reynolds number (Re) for laminar, turbulent, and transition regimes. The algorithm ensures convergence of both temperature and pressure before proceeding to the next segment increment.

theory: The documentation describes iterative procedures for pressure and temperature convergence in fluid flow. It provides correlations for internal (hi) and external (he) convective heat transfer coefficients and the calculation of total heat exchange including solar flux. For valves, the expansion is treated as an isenthalpic process (h_in = h_out) with outlet temperature determined via PH Flash. Flow coefficients (Kv) are defined by Linear, Quick Opening, Equal Percentage, or User-Defined relationships. Pumps increase liquid stream pressure based on adiabatic efficiency.

theory: Pumps increase liquid pressure using either a defined Delta-P or energy stream power. Equations involve enthalpy change (H2 = H1 + ΔP/ρ), power calculation (Pot = W(H2 - H1)/η), and discharge pressure (P2 = P1 + ΔP). Compressors/Expanders work on vapor streams based on an ideal isentropic process adjusted by efficiency. Power is calculated as P = W(H2s - H1)/η for compressors and P = (H2s - H1)*W*η for expanders. Coefficients for isentropic (ni) and polytropic (np) paths are calculated via ln(P2/P1)/ln(ρ2/ρ1). Head is calculated as H = P/(W*g).

steps: Access phase properties (Liquid1 and Liquid2) from the material stream (matstr).

Calculate volumetric fractions (vf) for each phase using phase volumetric flows.
Apply the emulsion equation: (mu_w/vf_w)*(1.0+1.5*mu_h*vf_h/(mu_w+mu_h)).
Assign the result to propval to update the mixture viscosity (Pa.s).
Re-run simulation to observe effects on pressure profiles.

params.CEPCI: Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index; components: equipment, labor, buildings, engineering
params.M&S Index: Marshall and Swift; composite of process and all-industry equipment averages
params.ENR Index: Engineering News-Record; general construction index (steel, cement, lumber, labor)

errors: Time gap > 10 years - Cost escalation estimates lose accuracy beyond budget-level error margins
theory: Cost indexes adjust historical equipment/project costs to current values by accounting for inflation and price variation. Selection depends on industry; CEPCI and M&S are standard for chemical processes. Most indices have a reporting lag (approx. 3 months for CEPCI).

topic: Capital Cost Indexing and Location Factors

params.IPC: Intratec Chemical Plant Construction Index; includes 12-month forecast.
params.NF: Nelson-Farrar Indexes; specifically for petroleum and petrochemical refinery construction.
params.LF: Location Factor; dimensionless ratio adjusting cost relative to a basis (usually USGC).
params.Factorial Method: Capital cost estimation based on major equipment purchase cost multiplied by specific factors.

theory: Capital cost estimation utilizes time-based indexes (IPC, NF) to adjust historical costs. Geographic variance is addressed via the Location Factor (LF), where Cost_A = Cost_Basis * LF_A. LF can be estimated by adding 10% to the reference index for every 1000 miles from a major industrial hub. The Factorial Method derives total plant cost from individual equipment costs.

steps: Identify and size major equipment items from preliminary flowsheets.
Calculate total delivered cost (Ce) of major equipment.
Apply Lang factor (fL) to determine fixed capital cost (CfD) for quick estimates.
Refine accuracy by applying individual cost factors for erection, piping, electrical, instruments, buildings, and utilities.

params.CfD: Fixed capital cost of the project.
params.Ce: Total delivered cost of major equipment (tanks, reactors, columns, exchangers).
params.fL: Lang factor; process-dependent multiplier used to estimate total cost from equipment cost.

theory: The factorial method estimates fixed capital cost as a function of major equipment purchase costs. Basic Lang factor method (CfD = fL * Ce) provides early-stage estimates, while detailed factorial estimates break down costs into specific direct components like piping, electrical, and site preparation.

steps: Divide process into sub-units for detailed factor-based estimation.
Estimate direct capital costs for materials and installation labor.
Apply percentage factors for indirect costs based on direct capital.
Categorize operating costs into fixed and variable components for viability analysis.

params.Fixed_Operating_Costs: Maintenance, labor, lab costs, supervision, overheads, capital charges, taxes, insurance, royalties.
params.Variable_Operating_Costs: Raw materials, miscellaneous materials, utilities, shipping, packaging.

theory: Capital cost estimation utilizes factorial methods based on direct costs. Accuracy scales with the level of equipment detail and sub-unit identification. Operating costs assess viability via fixed (production-independent) and variable (production-dependent) expenses derived from flowsheets.

params.Fixed Costs: Expenses independent of production rate: maintenance, labor, lab costs, supervision, plant overheads, capital charges, taxes, insurance, licenses.
params.Variable Costs: Expenses proportional to production rate: raw materials, utilities, shipping, packaging, miscellaneous operating materials.
params.General Operating Expenses: Company-wide costs including R&D, sales, and general overheads; adds approximately 20-30% to direct production costs.

theory: Production costs are divided into direct site costs (fixed and variable) and general company operating expenses. Fixed costs are typically items outside plant supervision control, while variable costs are those for which supervision is held accountable. Total product cost includes site costs plus a 20-30% markup for corporate overheads, R&D, and sales.

DWSIM supports advanced simulation features including CAPE-OPEN compliance, Excel automation, aqueous electrolyte thermodynamics, neural network unit operations, pipe network analysis, and capital cost estimation.

steps: Create an error-free .dwsim simulation file.
Prepare an input XML file defining parameter changes and output requests.
Execute dwsim.exe with the -commandline argument and paths for simfile, input, and output.

params.-savechanges: Persists variable modifications to the .dwsim file.
params.-show <0|1>: Hides (0) or shows (1) calculation details window.
params.-locale <val>: Sets locale (en-US, pt-BR, es); defines decimal separators.
params.-input <path>: Path to XML file defining input parameters.
params.-output <path>: Path to XML file where results are written.

theory: Headless execution allows external software to optimize simulations by programmatically modifying variables and reading calculated properties via XML data exchange.

topic: Command Line Mode and XML Input Structure
steps: Place .dwsim file, input XML, and dwsim.exe in the same directory.
Run command: dwsim.exe -commandline -nosplash -show 1 -locale [lang] -simfile [file] -input [in.xml] -output [out.xml]
Configure simulation via <Configuration> node (Units, Pure Properties, Interaction Parameters).
Define object values in <InputParameters> and requested results in <OutputParameters>.

params.-input/output: XML files for input data and result storage.
params.UnitsSystem Mode: 'Default' uses simulation settings; 'Custom' requires a Name.
params.InteractionParameter: Defines binary interaction coefficients (kij) for property packages.
params.PureCompoundProperties: Overrides Molar_Weight, Critical_Temperature, Critical_Pressure, and Acentric_Factor.

theory: DWSIM supports headless execution via XML configuration, allowing external manipulation of compound properties, thermodynamic parameters, and simulation object variables for automated workflows.

steps: Select UnitsSystem mode (Default/Custom) and name.
Set Simulation NumberOfRuns and FormattedOutput for XML reporting.
Identify Compound String IDs from Pure Compound Properties Utility.
Define PureCompoundProperties (Name, Property ID, Value, Unit).
Configure PropertyPackage parameters and binary interaction coefficients (kij, A12, etc.).
Specify InputParameters (Object Name, Property ID, Value) to modify before simulation.

params.UnitsSystem/Mode: System of units selection (Default or Custom).
params.Compound/Name: String ID found in brackets in Pure Compound Properties Utility.
params.InteractionParameter: Binary interaction coefficients (kij, kji, A12, A21, alpha12) for specific compound pairs.
params.Property/ID: Identifier for the specific property to be modified/read.
params.InputParameters: Objects modified prior to simulation execution.

ui_paths: Pure Compound Properties Utility > compound selector combo box
theory: DWSIM utilizes XML-based configuration files to automate simulation runs by programmatically overriding pure component properties, thermodynamic interaction parameters, and unit operation inputs.

topic: Automation Interface and Property Package Configuration
steps: Define OutputParameters by specifying Object Name and Property ID to retrieve values post-simulation.
Configure Property Package via XML by setting ID attributes and values corresponding to flash tolerances and iteration limits.

params.PP_PHFILT/PSFILT/PTFILT: Internal loop tolerances for flash calculations.
params.PP_PHFMEI/PSFMEI/PTFMEI: Maximum external iterations for flash calculations.

theory: DWSIM automation uses XML-defined parameters to control the thermodynamics engine. Configuration involves setting convergence tolerances (PH/PS/PT flashes), maximum iterations, and selecting specific flash algorithms or EOS corrections (like Peneloux volume translation) to ensure simulation stability and accuracy.

topic: Pure Compound Property Parameters and Density Configuration

params.OS_LIQDENS: Flag (0/1) to use Equation of State for liquid density calculation.
params.PP_USE_EOS_VOLUME_SHIFT: Flag (0/1) to enable Peneloux volume translation correction.
params.Critical_Temperature/Pressure/Volume/Com: Critical point properties (Tc, Pc, Vc, Zc).
params.PR/SRK_Volume_Translation_Coefficient: Volume translation constants (si = ci/bi) for PR/SRK EOS.
params.IG_Entropy/Enthalpy/Gibbs_of_Formation_2: Standard state ideal gas formation properties at 25C.
params.Vapor_Pressure_Constant_EqNo/A-E: Constants for temperature-dependent vapor pressure equations.
params.Ideal_Gas_Heat_Capacity_Const_EqNo/A-E: Constants for ideal gas heat capacity (Cp) equations.
params.HVapA-D: Constants for heat of vaporization equations.

theory: DWSIM utilizes a standardized set of Parameter IDs to map pure component physical, critical, and thermodynamic properties. These include binary flags for density calculation methods (EOS vs. correlation) and coefficients for ChemSep-style temperature-dependent property equations (VP, Cp, Visc, Density).

topic: Compound Parameters, Binary Interaction Sets, and XML I/O Structure
steps: Define input variables in <InputParameters><SimulationObjects> using Property IDs and values.
Execute simulation via command line to process the XML file.
Retrieve calculated results from the <OutputParameters> section of the generated XML file.

params.Liquid_Density_Const_B-E: Constants for liquid density correlation equations.
params.HVapA-D: Constants for Heat of Vaporization equation.
params.UNIQUAC_R/Q: Molecular volume (R) and surface area (Q) parameters for UNIQUAC model.
params.kij: Binary interaction parameter for EOS models (PR, SRK, PC-SAFT, PRSV2).
params.NRTL A/B/C: NRTL interaction parameters: A (cal/mol), B (cal/mol.K), C (cal/mol.K²).
params.UNIQUAC A/B/C: UNIQUAC interaction parameters: A (cal/mol), B (cal/mol.K), C (cal/mol.K²).

theory: DWSIM maps thermodynamic property packages to specific Binary Interaction Parameter (IP) sets. EOS models (PR, SRK) primarily utilize kij values. Activity coefficient models (NRTL, UNIQUAC) utilize multi-term temperature-dependent parameters (A, B, and C). The XML interface allows automated simulation execution by mirroring input structures into an output format containing solved property values.

topic: Command Line Mode XML Structure and Property IDs
steps: Identify the Object Name and Property ID for the target variable
Format input data within the <InputParameters> XML section
Execute the simulation via command line
Extract results from the <OutputParameters> XML section

params.Property ID: Unique code defining an object property (e.g., PROP_MS_0 for Temperature)

theory: Command line mode enables automated simulation by interfacing with a structured XML file where objects and their attributes are identified by unique alphanumeric Property IDs.

steps: .NET: Reference DWSIM.Thermodynamics.dll in project; access via DTL.Thermodynamics.Calculator.
COM: Register using RegAsm.exe /tlb:DWSIM.Thermodynamics.tlb DWSIM.Thermodynamics.dll /codebase with Admin privileges.

params.PTFlash: Requires Property Package, compound names, mole fractions, T (K), P (Pa).
params.Output Matrix: (n+2)x3 string matrix containing phase names, phase mole fractions, and compound mole fractions.
params.GetPropPackList: Returns array of available property packages.
params.GetCompoundList: Returns array of available chemical compounds.

theory: DTL is a .NET/COM-callable DLL providing DWSIM's thermo engine. Calculates single compound/phase properties and 2/3-phase equilibrium flashes (PT, PH, PS, PVF, TVF).

topic: DTL Flash Calculation Matrix Output Structure

params.n: Number of compounds in the mixture
params.String Matrix: (n+2) x 3 or (n+3) x 3 array containing calculation results
params.PH, PS, TVF, PVF: Flash types returning an extra row for Temperature (K) or Pressure (Pa)

theory: DWSIM Thermodynamic Library (DTL) flash functions return data in a string matrix. Row 1: Phase names. Row 2: Phase mole fractions. Rows 3 to n+2: Compound mole fractions per phase. For specific flashes (PH, PS, TVF, PVF), the final row contains equilibrium T or P.

DWSIM is an open-source chemical process simulator featuring CAPE-OPEN support, property packages, aqueous electrolyte models, neural network and pipe network unit operations, and capital cost estimation.

steps: Install DWSIM Windows Desktop version.
In Excel, navigate to File > Options > Add-Ins > Manage > Go > Browse.
Select DWSIM.xll (32-bit) or DWSIM_64.xll (64-bit) from the DWSIM installation directory.
Access thermodynamic functions via the 'DWSIM' category in Excel.
Use Ctrl+Shift+Enter for functions returning arrays/matrices (e.g., PTFlash).

params.PTFlash: Calculates equilibrium; requires Property Package, Compound names, Mole Fractions, T (K), P (Pa).
params.GetPropPackList: Returns list of available Property Packages.
params.GetCompoundList: Returns list of compounds in the simulation.
params.Interaction Parameters: Optional overrides for internal DWSIM binary parameters.

ui_paths: File > Options > Add-Ins > Manage > Go > Browse
theory: Exposes DWSIM's internal thermodynamic engines to Excel. Supports single compound constant/temp-dependent properties, single-phase mixture properties, and Flash algorithms (PT, PH, PS, PVF, TVF).

topic: Excel Add-In Flash Calculations and Interaction Parameters
steps: Provide compound names, mole fractions, temperature (K), and pressure (Pa)
Optional: Provide n x n interaction parameter matrices to override defaults
Call flash function (PH, PS, TVF, or PVF)
Read results from the returned (n+2) x 4 matrix

params.Interaction Parameters: n x n matrices used to override internal Property Package values
params.Output Matrix: (n+2)x4 table: Row1=Phase names, Row2=Phase mole fractions, Rows3+=Compound mole fractions
params.kij: Interaction parameter used by PC-SAFT, PR, SRK, PR/LK, UNIFAC, LKP, COSMO-SAC
params.NRTL/UNIQUAC Constants: Includes Aij, Bij, Cij (cal/mol units) and Alpha for activity coefficient models

theory: Flash calculations via Excel return a matrix containing phase distributions. User-defined interaction parameters can override defaults for specific Property Packages (EOS or Activity Models) using structured n x n input matrices.

steps: Identify phase complexity (VLE, VLLE, SLE) of the system
Select flash algorithm based on mixture characteristics
Verify existence of Binary Interaction Parameters (BIPs)
Validate thermodynamic model suitability for the specific mixture

params.Nested Loops: Default for VLE; versions available for VLLE and SLE (Eutectic/Solid Solution)
params.Inside-Out: Optimized for petroleum; 3-phase option handles free water
params.Gibbs Minimization: Suitable for difficult, highly non-ideal chemical systems
params.3-phase immiscible: Fast VLLE algorithm assuming first compound is immiscible
params.Lee-Kesler-Plöcker: Property method for specific thermodynamic correlations
params.COSMO-SAC: Activity coefficient model for chemical systems

errors: Incorrect results - missing binary interaction parameters (BIPs)
Convergence failure - unsuitable flash algorithm for mixture type
Invalid predictions - inadequate background theory for selected model
theory: Flash Algorithms determine thermodynamic equilibrium phase distributions, amounts, and compositions at specific conditions (T, P, H, S). Complexity and calculation time increase with the number of co-existing phases (VLE < VLLE < SLE).

topic: Excel Add-In Thermodynamic Validation and Property Identifiers
steps: Select thermodynamic model and flash algorithm based on expected phase behavior (e.g., 3-phase for water/hydrocarbons).
Calculate Gibbs Free Energy (G = H - TS) using Excel Add-In functions.
Validate phase split by ensuring Gibbs Free Energy decreases; if no split, G remains constant.
errors: NotImplemented - Requested property is not available in DWSIM
fixes: Use 3-phase flash algorithms for immiscible mixtures like water/hydrocarbons.
Verify presence of Binary Interaction Parameters (BIPs) for chosen model.
theory: Flash calculations require suitable background theory, BIPs, and algorithms. Thermodynamic stability is confirmed when phase splits result in a decrease in Gibbs Free Energy (G = H - TS).

params.heatOfFusionAtNormalFreezingPoint: Enthalpy change on melting at 101325 Pa (J/mol)
params.heatOfVaporizationAtNormalBoilingPoint: Enthalpy change on vaporization at 101325 Pa (J/mol)
params.idealGasEnthalpyOfFormationAt25C: Ideal gas enthalpy of formation at 25C (J/mol)
params.standardFormationEnthalpyLiquid: Standard enthalpy of formation for liquid (J/mol)
params.cpAqueousInfiniteDilution: Heat capacity of solute in infinitely dilute aqueous solution (J/mol K)
params.fugacityCoefficientOfVapor: Vapor fugacity coefficient on saturation line
params.heatCapacityOfLiquid: Liquid Cp on saturation line (J/mol K)
params.heatOfFusion: Enthalpy change on fusion on melting line (J/mol)

theory: Identifiers and units for constant and temperature-dependent physical properties used in DWSIM compound databases for thermodynamic flash and property calculations.
Definitions for single compound and mixture properties used in thermodynamic calculations, including phase transitions, transport properties, and ideal gas behavior.

params.enthalpy/entropy: Energy/Entropy per mole or mass (J or J/K)
params.excessProperties: Excess H, S, G, A, U, V properties reflecting deviation from ideality
params.heatCapacityCp/Cv: Heat capacity at constant pressure or volume (J/K per mole/mass)
params.phaseFraction: Molar or mass fraction of fluid in a specific phase

theory: The DWSIM Excel Add-In exposes thermodynamic engine functions to compute state, transport, and phase equilibrium properties. Most calculations support both molar and mass bases across overall mixtures or specific phases.
